Extract and structure the following information as valid JSON only (no markdown, no extra text).Return ONLY this JSON structure:{"summary": "2-3 sentence summary of the video content","keyPoints": ["point 1", "point 2", ...],"aiConcepts": ["AI concept 1", "AI concept 2", ...],"technicalDetails": ["tool/framework 1", "implementation detail", ...],"visualContextFlags": [{"timestamp": 120, "reason": "Code demo", "context": "Shows Python implementation"}]}If the video is not about AI/tech, set aiConcepts and technicalDetails to empty arrays but still extract keyPoints.TRANSCRIPT:In the next few minutes, we will build an e-commerce chatbot without writing a single line of code. This chatbot will be able to handle everything related to our store, like product inquiries, order tracking, and dealing with returns and much, much more. And of course, you will be able to integrate this chatbot into any website as well, like Shopify, Squarespace, WordPress, Wix, as well as any custom website. If you're new to Chatling, it's a no-code platform that makes it super easy to build chatbots for any type of business. They also offer a free tier, which you can use to follow along with this video and get your first chatbot up and running today. If you sign up using the link in the description of this video, then you will be supporting my channel. First, let's have a look at what we'll be building in this video. This chatbot will be able to perform four important functions. First, it will be able to answer questions from our custom knowledge base, and this includes the products listed on our e-commerce website, as well as some other information about our business. This means that users can ask questions like, "I'm looking for a gaming mouse, any recommendations?" And our assistant gave us this response based on the actual listings on our website, and during the course of this video, I'll also show you how to include a direct link in this response to the product page. Our chatbot will also be able to track orders, so if the user asks something like, "What is my order status?" you will notice this little message saying that there was an intent match, and the intent matched order status. This means that instead of answering questions from a knowledge base, our chatbot effectively handed over to this order status flow, which we can see down here. And now the chatbot is asking us for an order number, so I'll just enter some random number, and this chatbot effectively reached out to an API endpoint, which we will implement in this video, and this returns some information about the delivery date of this order, and now we can either continue with the conversation or end it. The third feature is that our chatbot can also deal with returns and exchanges, so the user could enter something like, "I'm not happy with my order, it's defective, how can I return it?" We can now see that the intent matched this route called returns and exchange, and now the chatbot is asking the user whether they want to do a return or an exchange. Let's just say that we wanted to return this item. Now it will collect several information from us, so I'll just enter the order number, and now it's asking for the email address, and I'll just enter leonatest.com, now it's asking which items in the order we want to exchange, so let's just say all of them, and what's the reason? I'll just enter, it's defective, and at this stage it's telling us that it's locked the return, but what actually happened behind the scenes is that the chatbot sent an email to our support department to lock this return, and that takes us to the fourth feature of this chatbot, and that's the ability to hand this conversation over to a human agent. So let's say we had a conversation with the agent, and we just didn't find a suitable response. What we could do is ask something like, "Please can a human call me back?" or something in those lines, and we can see that the intent matched this human agent path, and now we can simply provide our email address, like leonatest.com, "I need more information about product XYZ," and at this stage our agent sent an email to the support team, and this email actually contains a lot of information about this conversation, which also includes the entire conversation history. Now let's have a look at building all of this from scratch. Head over to chatling.ai and log into your account, or use the link in the description of this video. From this dashboard, create a new chatbot by clicking on "Create chatbot," and from here you can select from a list of templates, which also includes an e-commerce starter, but we'll be creating our chatbot from scratch. Let's give our chatbot a name, I'll call it "Vault and Vector Agent," and Vault and Vector is the name of my fictitious e-commerce website. Then let's click on "Create." From this dashboard we can view all sorts of statistics about our chatbot, but if you are on the free plan some of this might be grayed out, but it really doesn't matter. You will still be able to create your chatbot nonetheless. Let's actually start with our knowledge base. To add a data source, click on "Add New," and now we can add all sorts of data sources. A very common use case for e-commerce websites are to simply scrape the website for all the product pages. So if you have a website you can simply enter the URL and then let the chat link do its thing. But what I prefer to do is to upload a document containing my product information. The results in my opinion are just way better than trying to scrape websites. So what I did is I created this text file and this simply contains the name, description, price, and a rating of each of these products. So you would get the product name, the description, you would also see the price, and a rating out of five. And I also created this PDF document that contains some information about the company, like their history, the mission statement, core values, contact information, etc. So all I have to do is select those files of my machine, then I can click on "Submit." So if I go back to our knowledge base, we can see that those documents are currently being processed, and after a few seconds our documents were processed and uploaded. Now let's create our chatbot. Let's click on "Boulder," and from here we can build our chatbot by adding blocks to the canvas. So if I click on "Blocks," we can send messages, we can capture information from the user, we can add AI responses, and a lot more. But before we add any blocks to this canvas, I first want to adjust a few settings. First, let's go to "AI Configuration," and let's click on "Instruction." We can use instructions to provide all sorts of information to our AI assistant, like their name, their role, their tone, etc. We can either manually enter those instructions, or we can click on "View Examples," and just select from one of these. I'm going to add this very first prompt, which says, "Your name is Joanne, and you are our customer support assistant." When referring to yourself, do not mention anything about being an AI assistant or AI model. Instead, refer to yourself as "Joanne." I'm going to add some extra instructions here, and this says, "When answering questions about product inquiries, please include a URL to the product page. The URL is simply this address, slash, the product name in lowercase with a dash in between the words." Now, of course, this would be unique to your website, so you can adjust this instruction specific to your use case. And that's actually all we have to change. Let's go to "Add Blocks," and let's start by creating a chatbot that can answer questions about our products. So, a simple rag chatbot. When the user starts the conversation, we want our assistant to send some welcome message. So, I'll call this group "Greeting," and for the text, let's simply enter something like, "Welcome to Vault and Victor." So, when the conversation starts, this message will pop up. So, let me show you this. When we click on "Preview," we can see that it takes a second, and then this welcome message appears. We can make this look way more natural by adding another text block within this group, and maybe this could say something like, "I am Joanne. How can I assist you today?" Now, if we preview this, we can see that greeting message show up, and after a second, we get this "I am Joanne" message. Now, at the moment, we can't send any messages. We can only start a new chat. In order to allow the user to input information, we can go to "Blocks," and with "In Capture Response," we have the ability to add buttons, we can capture text, email addresses, and a lot more. Let's add this text block. Let's rename this group to "Capture User Question," and let's attach the greeting to this new group. This capture text block will allow the user to enter information into an input field. Now, what we want to do is grab the text that the user entered, and store it in a variable that we can use throughout this flow. Luckily, that's super simple. Within "Save in Variable," we can select from a list of predefined variables, or we can create our own by entering any name for the variable, like "user_query," and then we can click on "Create" to create this variable. Next, we want to use a large language model to answer the user's question from the knowledge base. So, we can just go to "Add Blocks," let's add "AI Response," let's rename this group to get answer from knowledge base. Of course, let's attach this user question group to our new node. Then, if we click on this "AI" block, we can select a response structure from either knowledge base, or just from the AI model itself. Of course, we want to answer the question from the knowledge base, so we'll leave it on that. Then, for the question, we will use the variable that we created earlier. To select a variable, simply enter an opening curly brace. Then, in this list, we can view all the available variables, and what we're interested in is user_query. And believe it or not, that's all we need to build a RAG chatbot. Now, let's go ahead and preview this. So, in the chat, let's ask something like, "I'm looking for a gaming mouse," and we get our response back, and you will also notice that we get a URL to the product page, which of course doesn't exist, but you can see this is working. But this also ends the conversation. So, we actually can't ask any follow-up questions, and all we can do is start a new chat, which is not ideal. So, to allow the user to ask follow-up questions, all we have to do is grab this AI response block and loop it back around to capture text. So, it should look something like this. So, after we ask a question, you will notice that we are able to ask follow-up questions. Next, let's have a look at adding additional functionality to this chatbot, like the ability to track orders. By default, the chatbot will attempt to answer the question from the knowledge base, but in certain instances, the chatbot should realize that it needs to route the user down a different path. For instance, if the user's question is related to tracking an order, then it needs to route the user down the order tracking path. This is where intents come from. So, after the user submits the question, the flow will first check if there are any intents matching the user's keywords, and if not, it will continue with this next step. But if this text matches any intents within this flow, it will route the user down those intents path. To create a new intent, we can go to blocks, then under triggers, we can add the intent trigger. We can then select from all the available intents, and since we don't have any, we can create a new intent. We can start by giving our intent a name, like order status, and for the description, we can enter customer wants to know the status or location of their order. But the description is not all that important. What is important are sample phrases. The chatbot will try to match the user's question to these sample phrases, and if it's a close match, then it knows to route the user down this path. So, a common phrase could be something like, "Where is my order?" or maybe something like, "When will my order arrive?" Let's add a few more, like "Track my package." "What's the status of order number that?" "Has my order been shipped yet?" And I'll just add a few more. I would recommend adding as many phrases as possible, or at least until you get a score of 100 out of 100. Then let's create our intent. Then from the drop-down list, let's select our intent, and we now have an order status route. Now, to hook up this input field to our intent, we can simply click on this capture text block, then under match intent, click on add, and select order status. So, now after the user enters their question, the chatbot will first check if the question matches the intent, or otherwise continue answering questions from the knowledge base. Right, now let's continue building the intent path. The first thing we want to do is send some text back to the user. I'll just call this group prompt for order number. With the text, please enter your order number to check its status, and connect our intent trigger to this block. After asking for the order number, we need to provide the user some way of actually entering that number. So, under capture response, let's add this capture text block. Let's hook these up. Let's rename this group to capture and store order number. Within this capture text block, we can create a new variable, and let's call this order number, and let's create it. And now the order number will be stored in this variable, so we can use it later on in the flow. Now, it's very possible that the user could enter an invalid order number, or more specifically, a number that's not the correct length, or it doesn't contain the correct characters. So, we can use an LLM to validate that the number is indeed in the correct format. So, let's go to blocks, let's add AI response, let's rename this group to validate order number, and let's attach these two groups. Then, in the AI response, let's change this from knowledge base to AI model instead. And for the prompt, we can enter something like, you are an order number validator. Let's actually extend this a bit. You are an order number validator. Your task is to evaluate whether a given order number is valid and in the correct format. Then, we can simply specify the rules of the number formatting. But what is important is we are adding the order number variable here, so that our AI response has a view of it. Then, we're saying that if the order number is correct and meets all the criteria, then output the number one. If the order number is incorrect or fails to meet the criteria, then output the number zero. And very important, your response should only consist of the digit zero or one without anything else. So, I do want to show you what that looks like. So, let's go to preview, let's enter something like, what is my order status? And we can see that we are in this contained path now. We can see that this is valid because technically it is following our criteria, but let's try something that's invalid. So, let's just ask again, what is my order status? And let's enter something that's invalid. So, I'll add spices and let's save this. And this time we are seeing a zero, which means that this number is invalid. Now, obviously we don't want to show that one or zero to the user, so we can actually disable display response. Furthermore, we want to do something with that value. So, if the value is zero, we want to tell the user that the format is incorrect and ask them to try again. Or if the value is one, then we want to continue checking the order status. So, how can we do that? Well, it's quite easy. Within blocks, we can add a variable condition just below this AI response. With in configure condition, we can do routing based on the response generated by this AI model. Now, obviously we need to store this response in a variable so that we can use it within this block. So, back in the AI response node, let's go down to save response in variable. Let's give it a name like order number validation. Let's create this variable. And now the value of zero or one will be stored in this variable. This means that in our variable condition block, we can click on this condition label field. Let's call this valid order number. Then under the list of variables, let's select order number validation. And for the operator, let's select equals. And for the value, we'll check for one. So, that means that if the response being returned from the model is one, then we'll follow this path. Else, if it's not one, we'll follow this path. In fact, let's start with the else path. Let's go to blocks. Let's add a text block. Let's rename this group to re prompt. And for the text, let's enter the order number is invalid. Please check and enter a valid order number. So, we can now connect the else path to this re prompt group. And then we want the user to enter the number again. So, all we have to do is attach this group to the capture text block, like so. For the positive path, we want to retrieve the actual order status. There are many ways to do this, but realistically, you will most likely call an API endpoint. Now, I don't have an actual API for this demo. So, we will be using a mock API provided by Chatling. But in your application, you can just have a look at the documentation to find the exact APIs that you need to call. To add this block, go to blocks. And let's add the HTTP request block. And let's place it over here. Let's rename this group to retrieve order status. And I'm also going to attach this positive path to the retrieve order status group. Now, let's have a look at the contents of this block. I'm going to use this mock API that is provided by Chatling. And you can find this endpoint by looking at the ecommerce template provided by Chatling. Then under headers, let's add a new header. Let's add this authorization property. And let's also add in this bearer token. This is needed to securely authenticate this API call. And chances are your platform will also require some sort of authentication. Then under body, let's add the new value called order number. And now we can pass in the variable that we created earlier called order number. And finally, we want to get the response back from the service. So under capture response, let's add a new field called message. And now we can store this message in a new variable called order underscore status. And let's create this variable. This was by far the most complicated step in this entire flow. And everything else will seem very simple in comparison. Now this service could be successful or it could fail. As an example, the API endpoint could be unavailable or some other technical failure occurred. So let's actually deal with a negative path first. If the call to the service failed, we can ask the user to retry. So let's add a new text block. We can rename this group to ask to retry. Let's send the text. I couldn't fetch the status of your order. Would you like to try again? And let's add a few buttons to allow the user to say yes or no. So let's add buttons. Let's add a below this text. And for button one, let's simply enter yes. And let's add another button with the label of no. And let's also attach this file path to ask to retry. And if the user clicks on yes, then we can simply loop them all the way back to prompt for order number. Restarting this process. If the user clicks on no, then our chatbot can ask them a user friendly question, like, is there anything else I can assist you with? Let's also rename this group to confirmation. Let's attach this no button to this confirmation group. And again, we want to give the user some ability to select yes or no. So as you would have guessed, we'll add buttons. The first button being yes, or the second button being no. And the reason I'm adding this confirmation group and not simply ending the conversation outright is we will reuse this confirmation group for some of the other intents as well. It just creates a user friendly experience instead of terminating the chat outright. Now, if the user says no, they don't need assistance with anything else, then it's safe to terminate this conversation. But we just don't want to end it abruptly. But we want our agent to give some friendly message. So at least attaches no button to the int chat group. And for the text, we can enter, thank you for chatting with us today. If you have any questions, feel free to start a new chat. I don't know about you, but I think that's a friendly and suitable way to end the conversation. So if the user selects yes, it means they want to continue with the conversation and maybe ask follow up questions. So it would make sense then to take them back to this initial capture text node. So all we have to do is attach this yes button to the capture user's question group. And now they can do things like ask questions about products and whatever else. Now, that was a little bit of a detour, but these two buttons are important for the rest of this flow. But of course, what do we do if we were able to retrieve the order status? Well, that's quite simple. We simply want to show the response to the user. So let's go to blocks. Let's add a new text block. Let's attach this success path to our new group. Let's call this group display order status. And for the text, all we have to do is show the variable order status, like so. And just as a refresher, order status is populated from this API call. So if we go to response, we can see that message was indeed saved in order status. Now, after we show the order status, I think it would make sense to show this confirmation message asking the user if there's anything else they need assistance with. Let's go ahead and test this. So in preview, let's ask what is my order status, then let's add a valid order number. We can see the response that we got back from the API call. And we can see that confirmation message. And if we click on yes, we will be redirected back to the Scapture user question group. And we can ask follow up questions. Let's work on the returns intent. So this has been the user asks to return or exchange their items. Let's go to add blocks, then let's add a new intent trigger. Then under intent, let's create a new intent. And let's call this return slash exchange request for the description. Let's enter customer wants to return or exchange a product. Let's add a few phrases like how do I return this item, or I want to return my purchase. After adding a few phrases, we can then create our intent, then it's also selected from this drop down. And very important, we need to go back to our capture users question input. And under matching 10, let's also select the return exchange request in 10. Now let's go ahead and build a same 10 route. Let's start by adding a text block. Let's rename this group to confirm return or exchange for the text. Let's enter do you want to return or exchange an item? Now we want to show a few buttons. So let's go to buttons, let's add a pillow this text. And for button one, we can enter return for button two, it's enter exchange. And for button three, it's enter none is also store the user's choice in a new variable called return type. And let's create this variable. Now if the user selects none, we can actually just redirect them back to this confirmation group. So I'm actually just going to grab none and attach it to the confirmation group. If the user selects return or exchange, we want to collect some information from the user. So let's go to blocks, let's add text, let's rename the group to get all the number. And for the initial text, let's enter what's the order number. Let's also go ahead and attach return as well as exchange to the get all the number group. Now after asking the user for the order number, we need the user to provide the order number. So let's add a text block, then let's store the value of the order number in a variable. And this time, we actually don't have to create a variable because we've already created an order number variable. Let's also ask the user for the email address. So add some text and let's change this to watch your email address, then under add blocks, let's add the email response, and then under saving variable, let's select one of the existing variables called contact email. Then it's followed us up with some more text like which items from the order do you want to return or exchange. Then of course, we have to capture the response from the user. So let's add a text input. Let's create a new variable for this called items for return. Let's create this. And finally, let's ask the user what's the reason and let's add a text input just below this. Let's store the reason in a new variable called return reason. Let's create this. And now that we've collected all this information, we can send an email to support. So to send an email from chatling, we can go to blocks, then let's add this send email block. Let's rename this to email support and let's click on configure email. Let's change the from name to support chatbot. Under reply to let's add curly braces, let's add contact email, and let's click on add. So this simply means that our support will be able to respond directly to the customer. And then the to address we can make something like Leon at vault vector dot IO, or something like that. And we can

editmore_vert{"summary": "This tutorial demonstrates how to build a no-code e-commerce chatbot using the Chatling.ai platform, capable of handling product inquiries, order tracking, and returns. It covers integrating a custom knowledge base, setting up intent-based routing, and connecting to external APIs for real-time data retrieval.","keyPoints": ["Introduction to Chatling.ai as a no-code chatbot builder for e-commerce platforms like Shopify and WordPress.","Setting up a knowledge base by uploading TXT and PDF documents for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG).","Configuring AI personas and instructions to define the chatbot's name, role, and response formatting.","Using intent triggers to route users to specific flows like order tracking or returns based on sample phrases.","Implementing LLM-based input validation to verify order number formats before processing requests.","Integrating external API endpoints via HTTP Request blocks to fetch live order status data.","Automating support processes by collecting user data and sending emails to human agents or support departments."],"aiConcepts": ["RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)","Knowledge Base","Intent Recognition/Matching","LLM Input Validation","Prompt Engineering","Persona/System Instructions"],"technicalDetails": ["Chatling.ai","HTTP Request Block","Bearer Token Authentication","Mock API integration","Variable storage and conditional routing","PDF/TXT data ingestion","Intent Sample Phrases (NLU scoring)"],"visualContextFlags": [{"timestamp": 60, "reason": "Functionality demo", "context": "Shows the chatbot handling order status and intent matching"},{"timestamp": 225, "reason": "Knowledge base configuration", "context": "Demonstrates uploading TXT and PDF files for data sources"},{"timestamp": 330, "reason": "System prompt setup", "context": "Shows configuration of AI instructions and URL formatting rules"},{"timestamp": 405, "reason": "Flow builder canvas", "context": "Visual demonstration of adding blocks and connecting nodes"},{"timestamp": 600, "reason": "Intent management", "context": "Displaying the creation of sample phrases for intent triggers"},{"timestamp": 750, "reason": "Validation logic", "context": "Shows LLM prompt used to validate order numbers with 0/1 output"},{"timestamp": 960, "reason": "API integration", "context":
